project: High-Resolution Real-time Deforestation Monitoring via Multimodal Satellite Fusion

records.content: Good morning Prof. Silva. I've finished the first draft of my research proposal. I'm aiming for an ambitious global monitoring system using Sentinel-2 optical data. My goal is to build a unified U-Net classifier to detect forest loss across the entire tropical belt. I've attached the V1 draft.

Alex, the ambition in f1.pdf is noted, but 'global' is a trap for a Master's thesis. Tropical forests in Southeast Asia have very different spectral signatures compared to the Amazon due to soil and species variation. You'll spend more time gathering labels than doing research. I strongly suggest you narrow the scope to the Amazon basin. More importantly, have you considered the cloud coverage bottleneck? Optical sensors are blind for nearly 6 months of the year during the peak burning season.

project: High-Resolution Real-time Deforestation Monitoring via Multimodal Satellite Fusion

records.content: I've taken your advice. The revised proposal in f2.pdf focuses solely on the Amazon. I'm specifically targeting cattle ranching—it's the primary driver here and shows a distinct temporal signature. I've switched to a ResNet-LSTM to capture the canopy's evolution over time.

The focus in f2.pdf on cattle ranching is much better and academically defensible. However, the 'Amazon Cloud Problem' is still unaddressed. A temporal LSTM will just have 'missing' data for months during the wet season. If you really want to make this 'real-time,' you must look into Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR). Sentinel-1 can penetrate clouds. How will you fuse SAR's structural data with Sentinel-2's spectral data? That's the core methodology question you need to solve.
